toxicity_notes: Generally non-toxic; flowers and hips are edible; no significant documented toxicity.

soil_type: well-drained loam, tolerant of poor soils
toxicity_notes: Generally non-toxic; hips edible; no significant documented toxicity.
native_regions: Native to central and southern Europe and western Asia.

toxicity_notes: Seeds/pits, leaves, and bark contain cyanogenic glycosides (amygdalin); toxic to humans and pets if chewed/crushed in quantity, releasing cyanide.
Seeds/pits, leaves, and bark contain cyanogenic glycosides (amygdalin); toxic to humans and pets if chewed/crushed in quantity, releasing cyanide. Wilting leaves especially dangerous to livestock.
native_regions: Native to Europe and western Asia.

toxicity_notes: Seeds/pits, leaves, and bark contain cyanogenic glycosides (amygdalin); toxic to humans and pets if chewed/crushed in quantity, releasing cyanide.
native_regions: Likely of hybrid origin (Prunus cerasifera x Prunus spinosa); associated with the Caucasus/western Asia region, naturalized and cultivated across Europe since antiquity.

toxicity_notes: Bitter almond varieties contain high amygdalin and are toxic raw (release cyanide); sweet (commercial) almonds are safe to eat, but leaves, bark, and seed coat still contain cyanogenic compounds and pose risk if consumed in quantity.
native_regions: Native to the Middle East, Iran, and Central Asia.

toxicity_notes: Seeds contain amygdalin (a cyanogenic glycoside); toxic in large quantities if chewed/crushed, releasing cyanide. Flesh of fruit is safe.
native_regions: Native to Central Asia (Tian Shan mountains, Kazakhstan), derived largely from wild ancestor Malus sieversii.

toxicity_notes: Fruit generally non-toxic and edible; seeds contain trace amygdalin but not considered a significant hazard in typical consumption.
native_regions: Native to Europe and western Asia.

soil_type: well-drained sandy loam, rich in organic matter
toxicity_notes: Fruit and leaves generally non-toxic; widely consumed and considered safe.
native_regions: Garden hybrid origin (Fragaria virginiana x Fragaria chiloensis); Fragaria virginiana native to North America, Fragaria chiloensis native to the Pacific coast of the Americas including Chile. Not native to Paraguay/most of South America as a species — it is a horticultural hybrid, though one parent (F. chiloensis) is native to coastal Chile.

soil_type: well-drained loam, rich in organic matter
toxicity_notes: Fruit non-toxic and edible; leaves sometimes used in herbal tea; no significant documented toxicity.
native_regions: Native to Europe and northern Asia (temperate regions).

soil_type: tolerant of a wide range of soils, well-drained preferred
toxicity_notes: Fruit non-toxic and edible; leaves and canes not documented as toxic; wilted leaves of related Rubus species occasionally noted with trace cyanogenic compounds but not a major concern.
native_regions: Native to Europe. Documented as a highly aggressive invasive species (as part of the Rubus fruticosus aggregate/Himalayan blackberry complex) in North America, Australia, New Zealand, and parts of South America (e.g., Chile).

soil_type: tolerant of a wide range of soils, well-drained preferred
toxicity_notes: Fruit (haws), flowers, and leaves generally non-toxic and used in traditional herbal remedies; seeds contain trace cyanogenic compounds but not considered a significant hazard.
native_regions: Native to Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Documented environmental weed/invasive in parts of North America (especially Pacific coast), Australia, and New Zealand, forming dense bird-dispersed thickets in pastures and forest understory.

soil_type: tolerant of a wide range of soils, moist well-drained preferred
toxicity_notes: No significant documented toxicity to humans or pets.
native_regions: Native to Japan, China, and Korea. Documented invasive species in the eastern United States (e.g., Virginia, Georgia, South Carolina, Tennessee, Kentucky, Great Smoky Mountains), escaping cultivation and forming dense stands in disturbed areas, fields, forests, and stream banks.

common_names: Shrubby cinquefoil|Bush cinquefoil (current accepted name Dasiphora fruticosa)
soil_type: tolerant of a wide range of soils, well-drained preferred
toxicity_notes: No significant documented toxicity to humans or pets.
native_regions: Native to circumboreal temperate and subarctic regions of the Northern Hemisphere (Europe, Asia, North America).

toxicity_notes: Leaves contain cyanogenic glycosides and can release hydrogen cyanide when chewed/crushed; low risk to humans but documented as potentially toxic to pets/livestock (especially ruminants) if consumed in quantity.
native_regions: Horticultural hybrid (Photinia glabra x Photinia serratifolia/serrulata), parent species native to Japan and China; sterile hybrid, not known to be invasive.
